title: ADR: PostgreSQL vs MongoDB
description: Decision between relational (PostgreSQL) and document-oriented (MongoDB) databases. PostgreSQL excels at complex queries, ACID transactions, and relational data. MongoDB excels at flexible schemas, horizontal scaling, and document-oriented data.
best_practices: Choose PostgreSQL for complex relationships and transactions
Choose MongoDB for flexible schemas and document-centric data
Consider hybrid approach for different data needs
Document the decision rationale

title: ADR: Kafka vs RabbitMQ
description: Decision between Apache Kafka (distributed log) and RabbitMQ (message broker). Kafka excels at high-throughput event streaming and replay. RabbitMQ excels at complex routing, priority queues, and traditional messaging.
best_practices: Choose Kafka for event streaming and high throughput
Choose RabbitMQ for complex routing and traditional messaging
Consider operational complexity
Evaluate message ordering requirements
anti_patterns: Using Kafka as a simple task queue

title: ADR: Monolith vs Microservices
description: Decision between monolithic and microservices architecture. Monoliths are simpler to develop, deploy, and debug. Microservices offer independent scaling, deployment, and technology diversity but add distributed system complexity.
best_practices: Start with monolith for new products
Extract microservices when clear boundaries emerge
Consider team size and organizational structure
Use modular monolith as stepping stone

title: ADR: JWT vs Session Authentication
description: Decision between stateless JWT tokens and server-side sessions. JWT enables stateless auth and is ideal for distributed systems. Sessions provide easier revocation and smaller request sizes.
best_practices: Use JWT for stateless microservices
Use sessions for traditional web apps
Implement token refresh mechanism
Keep JWT payloads small

title: ADR: REST vs GraphQL
description: Decision between REST and GraphQL API styles. REST is simpler and well-suited for resource-oriented APIs. GraphQL reduces over-fetching and enables flexible queries but adds complexity.
best_practices: Use REST for simple CRUD operations
Use GraphQL for complex data fetching needs
Consider BFF pattern as middle ground
Evaluate caching implications
anti_patterns: No query complexity limits in GraphQL

title: ADR: SQL vs NoSQL for Event Store
description: Decision on storage technology for event sourcing. SQL databases provide ACID guarantees and familiar tooling. Dedicated event stores (EventStoreDB) provide optimized event handling.
best_practices: Use dedicated event store for event-heavy systems
Use SQL for simpler event sourcing needs
Consider append-only table design
Plan for event schema evolution
anti_patterns: Using document DB for event ordering

title: ADR: Serverless vs Containers
description: Decision between serverless (Lambda/Cloud Functions) and container-based deployment. Serverless minimizes ops overhead with per-invocation pricing. Containers provide more control and predictable performance.
best_practices: Use serverless for event-driven workloads
Use containers for long-running services
Consider cold start impact
Evaluate cost at different scales

title: ADR: Terraform vs CloudFormation
description: Decision between multi-cloud Terraform and AWS-native CloudFormation for IaC. Terraform offers multi-cloud support and HCL language. CloudFormation provides deep AWS integration and native support.
best_practices: Use Terraform for multi-cloud or cloud-agnostic strategies
Use CloudFormation for AWS-only with deep integration needs
Use CDK for programmatic infrastructure
Maintain state management practices

title: ADR: Synchronous vs Asynchronous Communication
description: Decision between sync (HTTP/gRPC) and async (message queues/events) communication between services. Sync is simpler but creates coupling. Async enables decoupling and resilience.
best_practices: Use sync for queries requiring immediate response
Use async for commands and events
Implement compensating transactions for async flows
Design for idempotency

title: ADR: Centralized vs Distributed Logging
description: Decision on logging architecture. Centralized logging aggregates all logs into a single system (ELK, CloudWatch). Distributed keeps logs local but requires tooling for cross-service queries.
best_practices: Use centralized logging for production
Implement structured logging format
Include correlation IDs
Set retention policies
